article.subject: Indian Geography
article.title: The Physical Divisions of India
article.author: Tawqeer ul Islam
article.summary: The first part of an in-depth series on India’s physiography, focusing on the origin, structural composition, and regional classification of the Great Himalayan Range.
article.content:
India’s physical landscape is a result of millions of years of geological processes, primarily the northward drift of the Indian Plate and its subsequent collision with the Eurasian Plate. This tectonic interaction created a land of extremes. To understand India's geography, we must categorize it into six distinct physiographic divisions. In this first installment, we explore the "Crown of India"—the Himalayas.

Figure 1: The six major physical divisions of India. Source: Wikimedia Commons.

The Himalayan Mountains: The Northern Sentinel

The Himalayas are geologically 'young' and structurally 'fold' mountains. They represent the loftiest and one of the most rugged mountain barriers in the world. They form an arc, which covers a distance of about 2,400 km. Their width varies from 400 km in Kashmir to 150 km in Arunachal Pradesh.

1. Geological Origin

According to the Theory of Plate Tectonics, the Himalayas were formed when the Indo-Australian plate collided with the Eurasian plate. The sedimentary rocks which were accumulated in the geosyncline known as the Tethys were folded to form the mountain system of western Asia and the Himalayas.

Figure 2: Animated representation of the Indian Plate's collision. Source: Wikimedia Commons.

2. Longitudinal Divisions

The Himalayas consist of three parallel ranges in its longitudinal extent:

  • The Great or Inner Himalayas (Himadri): This is the most continuous range consisting of the loftiest peaks with an average height of 6,000 metres. It contains all prominent Himalayan peaks such as Mt. Everest, Kanchenjunga, and Nanga Parbat. The core of this part is composed of granite.
  • The Lesser Himalaya (Himachal): Lying to the south of the Himadri, this range is mainly composed of highly compressed and altered rocks. The altitude varies between 3,700 and 4,500 metres. Important ranges here include the Pir Panjal (the longest), the Dhauladhar, and the Mahabharat ranges.
  • The Shiwaliks: The outermost range of the Himalayas. They extend over a width of 10-50 km and have an altitude varying between 900 and 1100 metres. These ranges are composed of unconsolidated sediments. The longitudinal valleys lying between the Himachal and the Shiwaliks are known as Duns (e.g., Dehra Dun, Kotli Dun).

3. Regional Divisions (West to East)

Besides the longitudinal divisions, the Himalayas have been divided on the basis of regions from west to east, demarcated by river valleys:

  • Punjab Himalayas: Between the Indus and Satluj rivers.
  • Kumaon Himalayas: Between the Satluj and Kali rivers.
  • Nepal Himalayas: Between the Kali and Teesta rivers.
  • Assam Himalayas: Between the Teesta and Dihang (Brahmaputra) rivers.
  • Purvachal: Beyond the Dihang gorge, the Himalayas bend sharply to the south and spread along the eastern boundary of India, known as the Purvachal or Eastern hills (Patkai hills, Naga hills, Manipur hills, and Mizo hills).
Figure 3: Kanchenjunga, the highest peak in India, located in the Himadri range. Source: Wikimedia Commons.

The Himalayas act as a climatic divide, preventing the cold Central Asian winds from entering the Indian subcontinent while trapping the Monsoon winds to provide rain. In the next section, we will descend from these heights to the fertile Northern Plains.

2. The Northern Plains: The Granary of India

The Northern Plains were formed by the alluvial deposits brought by three major river systems—the Indus, the Ganga, and the Brahmaputra—along with their tributaries. This plain is about 2,400 km long and 240 to 320 km broad. It is a geologically recent feature and is incredibly fertile, making it the agricultural heartland of India.

Figure 4: The vast, fertile stretch of the Indo-Gangetic Plain. Source: Wikimedia Commons.

Geomorphological Variations

The Northern Plains are not just flat lands; they exhibit significant relief features and are divided into four specific regions:

  • Bhabar: As rivers descend from the mountains, they deposit pebbles in a narrow belt (8-16 km wide) parallel to the Shiwaliks. In this region, most streams disappear underground.
  • Terai: South of the Bhabar, the streams re-emerge, creating a wet, swampy, and marshy region known as the Terai. Historically, this was a thickly forested region rich in wildlife.
  • Bhangar: This is the older alluvium of the plains, lying above the floodplains of the rivers. It presents a terrace-like feature. The soil here contains calcareous deposits locally known as Kankar.
  • Khadar: The newer, younger deposits of the floodplains. They are renewed almost every year by floods and are therefore highly fertile, ideal for intensive agriculture.

Regional Divisions

The plain is broadly divided into three sections: The Punjab Plains (dominated by the Indus and its five tributaries), the Ganga Plains (extending between Ghaggar and Teesta rivers), and the Brahmaputra Plain (lying mainly in Assam).

3. The Peninsular Plateau: The Ancient Tableland

The Peninsular Plateau is a tableland composed of the old crystalline, igneous, and metamorphic rocks. It was formed by the breaking and drifting of the Gondwana land, making it one of the oldest and most stable landmasses on Earth. It is characterized by broad, shallow valleys and rounded hills.

Figure 5: Map illustrating the extent of the Deccan Plateau. Source: Wikimedia Commons.

Major Sub-divisions

  • The Central Highlands: Lying to the north of the Narmada river, this part covers a major area of the Malwa plateau. The Vindhyan range is bounded by the Central Highlands on the south and the Aravallis on the northwest. Further eastward extensions are known as Bundelkhand and Baghelkhand, ending at the mineral-rich Chota Nagpur plateau.
  • The Deccan Plateau: A triangular landmass south of the Narmada. It is higher in the west and slopes gently eastwards. An extension of the plateau is also visible in the northeast, locally known as the Meghalaya and Karbi-Anglong Plateau.

The Western and Eastern Ghats

The edges of the Deccan Plateau are marked by the Western and Eastern Ghats:

Feature Western Ghats (Sahyadri) Eastern Ghats
Continuity Continuous; can be crossed through passes only. Discontinuous and irregular; dissected by rivers.
Elevation Higher (900–1600 metres). Lower (Average 600 metres).
Highest Peak Anamudi (2,695 m). Mahendragiri (1,501 m).

A distinct feature of the Peninsular plateau is the Deccan Trap—a black soil area of volcanic origin, which is exceptionally good for cotton cultivation.

4. The Great Indian Desert (Thar Desert)

The Great Indian Desert, also known as the Thar Desert, lies towards the western margins of the Aravalli Hills. It is an undulating sandy plain covered with sand dunes. This region is a land of extreme temperatures and scarce vegetation, acting as a natural boundary between India and Pakistan.

Figure 6: The shifting sand dunes of the Thar Desert, Rajasthan. Source: Wikimedia Commons.

Key Characteristics

  • Climate: The region receives very low rainfall, below 150 mm per year, resulting in an arid climate with low vegetation cover.
  • Hydrology: Streams appear only during the rainy season. Shortly after, they disappear into the sand as they do not have enough water to reach the sea. The Luni is the only large river in this region.
  • Barchans: Crescent-shaped sand dunes called Barchans cover large areas, but longitudinal dunes become more prominent near the Indo-Pakistan border.

5. The Coastal Plains

The Peninsular plateau is flanked by a strip of narrow coastal plains, running along the Arabian Sea on the west and the Bay of Bengal on the east. These plains are vital for India's maritime trade, fishing, and agriculture.

Figure 7: The Malabar Coast is famous for its extensive network of backwaters. Source: Wikimedia Commons.

The Western Coastal Plain

Sandwiched between the Western Ghats and the Arabian Sea, it is a narrow plain consisting of three sections:

  • Konkan: The northern part (Mumbai – Goa).
  • Kannad Plain: The central stretch.
  • Malabar Coast: The southern stretch, famous for its lagoons and backwaters.

The Eastern Coastal Plain

The plains along the Bay of Bengal are wide and level. In the northern part, it is referred to as the Northern Circar, while the southern part is known as the Coromandel Coast. Large rivers such as the Mahanadi, Godavari, Krishna, and Kaveri have formed extensive deltas on this coast. Lake Chilika is an important feature along the eastern coast.

6. The Islands

India has two main groups of islands, which differ significantly in their geological origin and structure.

Figure 8: Aerial view of the Andaman and Nicobar archipelago. Source: Wikimedia Commons.

Lakshadweep Islands

Located in the Arabian Sea close to the Malabar coast of Kerala. This group of islands is composed of small coral islands. They cover a small area of 32 sq km. Kavaratti island is the administrative headquarters. This island group has great diversity of flora and fauna, including the Pitti Island, which is a bird sanctuary.

Andaman and Nicobar Islands

This is an elongated chain of islands located in the Bay of Bengal extending from north to south. They are bigger in size and are more numerous and scattered. These islands are believed to be an elevated portion of submarine mountains. They are of great strategic importance for the country and experience an equatorial climate with thick forest cover.

Conclusion: A Geographical Synthesis

A detailed survey of the physical divisions of India reveals that each region complements the others. The mountains are the major sources of water and forest wealth. The northern plains are the granaries of the country, providing the base for early civilizations. The plateau is a storehouse of minerals, which has played a crucial role in the industrialization of the country. Finally, the coastal regions and island groups provide sites for fishing and port activities. Thus, the diverse physical features of the land have immense future possibilities of development.

article.subject: Indian Geography
article.title: Political Divisions of India
article.author: Tawqeer ul Islam
article.summary: A comprehensive guide to India's political structure, focusing on state reorganizations, parliamentary constituencies, and a detailed profile of Northern India as per recent JKSSB exam trends.
article.content:
India, a Sovereign Socialist Secular Democratic Republic, is a Union of States as per Article 1 of the Constitution. For administrative convenience, it is currently divided into 28 States and 8 Union Territories. Understanding the political map is crucial for competitive exams, especially concerning the specific seats in the Parliament and the history of state formation.

Figure 1: Current Political Map of India (28 States & 8 UTs). Source: Wikimedia Commons.

1. National Administrative Statistics

  • Total Lok Sabha Seats: 543
  • Total Rajya Sabha Seats: 245
  • States with Bicameral Legislature (Legislative Council): 6 (Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, Maharashtra, Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, Telangana).
  • Standard Meridian of India: 82°30'E (passes through Mirzapur, UP).

2. Focus: Northern India (The Himalayan & Frontier States)

This region is of paramount importance for JKSSB aspirants, especially regarding the J&K Reorganisation Act, 2019.

A. Jammu & Kashmir (Union Territory)

  • Formation Date: 31st October 2019 (as a UT).
  • Capitals: Srinagar (Summer), Jammu (Winter).
  • Judicial Capital: High Court of J&K and Ladakh (Srinagar/Jammu).
  • Parliamentary Seats: Lok Sabha (5), Rajya Sabha (4).
  • Legislative Assembly: 90 seats (plus 24 seats reserved for PoK). No Legislative Council (abolished in 2019).
  • International Borders: Pakistan (Line of Control).
  • State/UT Borders: Ladakh (East), Himachal Pradesh (South), Punjab (South).

B. Ladakh (Union Territory)

  • Formation Date: 31st October 2019.
  • Administrative Capital: Leh and Kargil.
  • Parliamentary Seats: Lok Sabha (1), Rajya Sabha (0).
  • Legislature: No Legislative Assembly (Administered by LG).
  • International Borders: China (LAC), Pakistan (Gilgit-Baltistan), Afghanistan (Wakhan Corridor - via PoK).
  • Districts: Only 2 (Leh and Kargil).

C. Himachal Pradesh

  • Formation Date: 25th January 1971.
  • Capitals: Shimla (Summer), Dharamshala (Winter).
  • Judicial Capital: Shimla (High Court).
  • Parliamentary Seats: Lok Sabha (4), Rajya Sabha (3).
  • Assembly Seats: 68.
  • Borders: J&K, Ladakh, Punjab, Haryana, Uttarakhand. International: China (Tibet).
Figure 2: Geographical location of the newly formed UTs. Source: Wikimedia Commons.

D. Punjab & Haryana

Detail Punjab Haryana
Formation 1 Nov 1966 1 Nov 1966
Capital Chandigarh Chandigarh
Lok Sabha 13 10
Rajya Sabha 7 5
Assembly 117 90

E. Uttarakhand

  • Formation Date: 9th November 2000 (separated from UP).
  • Capitals: Dehradun (Winter), Gairsain (Summer).
  • Judicial Capital: High Court shifted from Nainital to Haldwani (recent development).
  • Parliamentary Seats: Lok Sabha (5), Rajya Sabha (3).
  • Assembly Seats: 70.
  • International Borders: Nepal, China.

In the next section, we will cover Western and Central India, focusing on states with the largest constituencies and those sharing maritime borders.

3. Western India: The Strategic Frontier

Western India is characterized by a mix of vast deserts and the country's longest coastline. It includes states of immense economic and strategic importance, particularly those sharing international borders with Pakistan.

Figure 3: The Rann of Kutch (Gujarat) - A unique marshy salt desert on the International Border. Source: Wikimedia Commons.

A. Rajasthan (The Largest State by Area)

  • Formation Date: 30th March 1949.
  • Capital: Jaipur.
  • Judicial Capital: Jodhpur (High Court).
  • Parliamentary Seats: Lok Sabha (25), Rajya Sabha (10).
  • Assembly Seats: 200 (Unicameral).
  • International Borders: Pakistan (Radcliffe Line - 1,070 km).
  • State Borders: Punjab, Haryana, Uttar Pradesh, Madhya Pradesh, Gujarat.

B. Gujarat (Longest Coastline)

  • Formation Date: 1st May 1960 (Separated from Bombay State).
  • Capital: Gandhinagar.
  • Judicial Capital: Ahmedabad (High Court).
  • Parliamentary Seats: Lok Sabha (26), Rajya Sabha (11).
  • Assembly Seats: 182.
  • International Borders: Pakistan (The disputed Sir Creek area).
  • Coastline: Approx 1,600 km (Longest in India).

C. Maharashtra (The Economic Powerhouse)

  • Formation Date: 1st May 1960.
  • Capitals: Mumbai (Summer), Nagpur (Winter).
  • Judicial Capital: Mumbai (High Court).
  • Parliamentary Seats: Lok Sabha (48 - 2nd highest in India), Rajya Sabha (19).
  • Legislature: Bicameral. Assembly (288 seats), Council (78 seats).
  • Borders: Gujarat, MP, Chhattisgarh, Telangana, Karnataka, Goa.

D. Goa (Smallest State by Area)

  • Formation Date: 30th May 1987 (Before this, it was a UT).
  • Capital: Panaji.
  • Judicial Capital: Under Bombay High Court (Panaji Bench).
  • Parliamentary Seats: Lok Sabha (2), Rajya Sabha (1).
  • Assembly Seats: 40.

4. Central India: The Heart of the Nation

Central India is landlocked and serves as a transition zone between the northern plains and the southern plateau. It is rich in minerals and tribal culture.

Figure 4: Madhya Pradesh is the only state sharing borders with 5 major states without an international border. Source: Wikimedia Commons.

A. Madhya Pradesh

  • Formation Date: 1st November 1956.
  • Capital: Bhopal.
  • Judicial Capital: Jabalpur (High Court).
  • Parliamentary Seats: Lok Sabha (29), Rajya Sabha (11).
  • Assembly Seats: 230.
  • Borders: UP, Rajasthan, Gujarat, Maharashtra, Chhattisgarh.
  • Note: Known as the 'Tiger State of India'.

B. Chhattisgarh

  • Formation Date: 1st November 2000 (Separated from MP).
  • Capital: Raipur.
  • Judicial Capital: Bilaspur (High Court).
  • Parliamentary Seats: Lok Sabha (11), Rajya Sabha (5).
  • Assembly Seats: 90.
  • Borders: MP, UP, Jharkhand, Odisha, Telangana, Maharashtra.

5. Focus Comparison: Political Strength

State LS Seats RS Seats Leg. Type Formation
Maharashtra 48 19 Bicameral 1960
Rajasthan 25 10 Unicameral 1949
Gujarat 26 11 Unicameral 1960

The next section will cover the high-density states of Eastern India and the "Seven Sisters" of the Northeast, which are frequent subjects of JKSSB questions regarding international borders and the 'Chicken's Neck' corridor.

6. Eastern India: The Land of Alluvial Wealth and Minerals

Eastern India consists of states with high population densities and significant political influence in the Parliament. This region is also home to India's major mineral belts and the largest delta in the world.

Figure 5: West Bengal is the only state sharing borders with three different countries (Nepal, Bhutan, Bangladesh). Source: Wikimedia Commons.

A. West Bengal

  • Formation Date: 26th January 1950.
  • Capital: Kolkata.
  • Judicial Capital: Kolkata (Calcutta High Court - oldest in India).
  • Parliamentary Seats: Lok Sabha (42), Rajya Sabha (16).
  • Assembly Seats: 294.
  • International Borders: Bangladesh (Longest), Nepal, Bhutan.
  • Geographic Note: Home to the Siliguri Corridor (Chicken's Neck), the narrow stretch connecting mainland India to the Northeast.

B. Bihar (Bicameral State)

  • Formation Date: 22nd March 1912 (Separated from Bengal); statehood in 1950.
  • Capital: Patna.
  • Judicial Capital: Patna (High Court).
  • Parliamentary Seats: Lok Sabha (40), Rajya Sabha (16).
  • Legislature: Bicameral. Assembly (243 seats), Council (75 seats).
  • International Borders: Nepal.

C. Jharkhand & Odisha

  • Jharkhand: Formed on 15th November 2000 (Birsa Munda's birth anniversary). Capital: Ranchi. LS Seats: 14. Assembly: 81.
  • Odisha: Formed on 1st April 1936 (Utkala Dibasa). Capital: Bhubaneswar. Judicial Capital: Cuttack. LS Seats: 21. Assembly: 147.

7. North-Eastern India: The Seven Sisters and Sikkim

The North-East is a region of immense strategic importance, connected to the rest of India by the Siliguri Corridor. For JKSSB aspirants, the international borders of these states are high-yield topics.

Figure 6: The North-Eastern states sharing borders with China, Myanmar, and Bangladesh. Source: Wikimedia Commons.

A. Sikkim (The Brother of the Seven Sisters)

  • Formation Date: 16th May 1975 (36th Amendment Act).
  • Capital: Gangtok.
  • Parliamentary Seats: Lok Sabha (1), Rajya Sabha (1).
  • Assembly Seats: 32 (Smallest assembly in India).
  • International Borders: China, Nepal, Bhutan. (Bordered by only one Indian state: West Bengal).

B. The Seven Sisters: Administrative Data

State Capital LS/RS Seats Formation International Borders
Assam Dispur 14 / 7 1950 Bhutan, Bangladesh
Arunachal Pradesh Itanagar 2 / 1 1987 China, Myanmar, Bhutan
Nagaland Kohima 1 / 1 1963 Myanmar
Manipur Imphal 2 / 1 1972 Myanmar
Mizoram Aizawl 1 / 1 1987 Myanmar, Bangladesh
Meghalaya Shillong 2 / 1 1972 Bangladesh
Tripura Agartala 2 / 1 1972 Bangladesh (Surrounded on 3 sides)

Quick Fact for JKSSB: The McMahon Line is the boundary between Arunachal Pradesh (India) and China, although China disputes this line.

In the final section, we will cover the Southern States (with their judicial capitals and bicameral legislatures) and the remaining Union Territories.

8. Southern India: The Peninsular States

Southern India is home to states with high literacy rates and significant economic contributions. This region contains most of India's remaining bicameral legislatures and unique judicial setups.

Figure 7: Map of the Southern Peninsular states. Source: Wikimedia Commons.

A. Andhra Pradesh & Telangana

  • Telangana: Formed on 2nd June 2014 (Separated from Andhra). Capital: Hyderabad. LS Seats: 17, RS Seats: 7. Assembly: 119, Council: 40.
  • Andhra Pradesh: Capital: Amaravati. LS Seats: 25, RS Seats: 11. Assembly: 175, Council: 58.
  • Judicial Capitals: Telangana (Hyderabad High Court); Andhra Pradesh (Andhra Pradesh High Court, Amaravati).

B. Karnataka (Bicameral)

  • Formation: 1st November 1956 (as Mysore State).
  • Capital: Bengaluru.
  • Parliamentary Seats: Lok Sabha (28), Rajya Sabha (12).
  • Legislature: Bicameral. Assembly (224), Council (75).
  • Borders: Maharashtra, Goa, Kerala, Tamil Nadu, Andhra Pradesh, Telangana.

C. Tamil Nadu & Kerala

  • Tamil Nadu: Capital: Chennai. LS Seats: 39, RS Seats: 18. Assembly: 234. Coastline: Coromandel Coast.
  • Kerala: Capital: Thiruvananthapuram. LS Seats: 20, RS Seats: 9. Assembly: 140. Coastline: Malabar Coast.

9. The Union Territories (UTs)

Under Article 239, Union Territories are administered by the President through an Administrator or Lieutenant Governor. There are currently 8 UTs in India.

Union Territory Capital LS/RS Seats Judicial Jurisdiction
NCT of Delhi New Delhi 7 / 3 Delhi High Court
Puducherry Puducherry 1 / 1 Madras High Court
Chandigarh Chandigarh 1 / 0 Punjab & Haryana High Court
D&N Haveli & Daman & Diu Daman 2 / 0 Bombay High Court
Andaman & Nicobar Port Blair 1 / 0 Calcutta High Court
Lakshadweep Kavaratti 1 / 0 Kerala High Court

Note for JKSSB: Dadra and Nagar Haveli and Daman and Diu were merged into a single UT on 26th January 2020.

Figure 8: Detailed administrative boundaries of India. Source: Wikimedia Commons.

Conclusion: The Dynamic Map of India

The political divisions of India are not static; they have evolved from the 562 princely states at the time of independence to the current 28 states and 8 UTs. Major milestones include the State Reorganisation Act (1956), the creation of smaller states in 2000 (Jharkhand, Uttarakhand, Chhattisgarh), and the landmark J&K Reorganisation Act (2019). For candidates appearing in JKSSB and other regional exams, mastering these administrative details—from the number of seats to the specific high court jurisdictions—is essential for a comprehensive understanding of Indian Polity.

In competitive examinations like JKSSB, questions often focus on extremes—the largest, the highest, and the most represented. This summary provides a quick-glance reference to the Top Five superlatives mentioned in our comprehensive study of India's Physical and Political divisions.

Source: Wikimedia Commons - Indian Administrative Limits.

1. Top 5 Largest States by Area

  1. Rajasthan: 342,239 sq km
  2. Madhya Pradesh: 308,252 sq km
  3. Maharashtra: 307,713 sq km
  4. Uttar Pradesh: 240,928 sq km
  5. Gujarat: 196,024 sq km

2. Top 5 States by Lok Sabha Seats (Political Power)

Rank State Seats
1Uttar Pradesh80
2Maharashtra48
3West Bengal42
4Bihar40
5Tamil Nadu39

3. Top 5 Highest Mountain Peaks in India

  • K2 (Godwin-Austen): 8,611 m (Located in Gilgit-Baltistan/PoK).
  • Kanchenjunga: 8,586 m (Sikkim - Highest in undisputed India).
  • Nanda Devi: 7,816 m (Uttarakhand).
  • Kamet: 7,756 m (Uttarakhand).
  • Saltoro Kangri: 7,742 m (Ladakh).

4. Top 5 Longest Coastlines (States)

Essential for questions on maritime geography:

  1. Gujarat: 1,600 km
  2. Andhra Pradesh: 974 km
  3. Tamil Nadu: 906 km
  4. Maharashtra: 720 km
  5. Kerala: 569 km

5. Top 5 Largest Union Territories by Area (Post-2019)

Crucial for JKSSB aspirants:

  1. Ladakh: 166,698 sq km (Largest UT)
  2. Jammu & Kashmir: 42,241 sq km
  3. Andaman & Nicobar Islands: 8,249 sq km
  4. Delhi (NCT): 1,483 sq km
  5. Dadra & Nagar Haveli and Daman & Diu: 603 sq km
Figure: K2, the highest point in the Indian Subcontinent. Source: Wikimedia Commons.

6. Top 5 International Borders by Length

  1. Bangladesh: 4,096.7 km
  2. China: 3,488 km
  3. Pakistan: 3,323 km
  4. Nepal: 1,751 km
  5. Myanmar: 1,643 km

Exam Tip: Remember the "Chicken's Neck" (Siliguri Corridor) is only about 22 km wide, and the Radcliffe Line (India-Pakistan) was drawn in 1947.
